system_description.overview: Minutes AI is an AI-powered meeting notetaker platform serving 600,000+ users across iOS, Android, macOS, Windows, and Web. It provides automated transcription, meeting notes, and AI-driven insights for enterprise and consumer users. The platform is rated 4.9 on the App Store.

products.AI Meeting Notetaker: AI-powered transcription and note-taking for meetings across all major platforms
products.AI Insights: AI-driven summaries, action items, and meeting analysis
products.Cross-platform apps: Native apps for iOS, Android, macOS, Windows, and Web

infrastructure.cloud_provider: Google Cloud Platform (GCP) — carve-out subservice organization (directly named, not via Supabase)
regions: Not explicitly named — GCP manages regions
infrastructure.availability_zones: Multi-AZ (disaster recovery spins up multiple servers across all availability zones in GCP)
serverless: Cloud Functions (GCP serverless compute — visible in network diagram)

network_architecture.network_segmentation: Network segmentation implemented to separate systems and data based on sensitivity and criticality (CC6.1.7 tested, no exceptions). Segmented VPCs and ACLs via GCP.
network_architecture.waf: Cloud Armor (GCP WAF — explicitly named and visible in network diagram; sits at the perimeter between Internet and Cloud Load Balancing)
network_architecture.firewalls: System firewalls configured on application gateway and production network to limit unnecessary ports, protocols, and services. Firewall rules reviewed annually by IT management (CC6.6.1, CC6.6.2, CC6.6.3 all tested, no exceptions).
network_architecture.ids_ips: Intrusion detection system utilized via GCP to monitor and detect unauthorized access or anomalies within the network (CC2.1.1 tested, no exceptions).
network_architecture.vpn: Remote access via bastion host with VPN.
network_architecture.tls: TLS using certificates from established third-party certificate authority (CC6.1.6 tested, no exceptions). TLS version not specified.
network_architecture.topology: Real network diagram (not a placeholder): Internet → Cloud Armor (WAF) → Cloud Load Balancing + Firebase Authentication → Cloud Functions (serverless compute) → Firestore (database) → Cloud Functions → GCP Security Command Center. VPC with segmented subnets. Bastion host with VPN for remote access.

application_architecture.pattern: Serverless-heavy (Cloud Functions as primary compute visible in network diagram)
compute: Cloud Functions (GCP serverless — visible in network diagram)
application_architecture.environments: Separate development, test/stage, and production environments. Changes tested in separate dev/test environment before production (CC8.1.4 tested, no exceptions). Environments are segregated.

databases: Firestore (GCP NoSQL document database — explicitly named and visible in network diagram)

authentication_access_control.identity_provider: Firebase Authentication (GCP — explicitly named and visible in network diagram)
authentication_access_control.mfa: MFA enforced for all user accounts with administrative access to the production platform (CC6.1.1 tested, no exceptions). MFA required for source code tool access (CC8.1.3). MFA enforced for all remote access (CC6.1.1 tested, no exceptions). 2FA referenced for email access.
authentication_access_control.access_revocation_sla: Within one business day upon termination (CC6.1.8 tested, no exceptions)
authentication_access_control.password_policy: Controlled through GCP password policy; unique user IDs and passwords required to gain access to application production environment and infrastructure (Active Directory, server, and database accounts); password settings for business-critical systems in accordance with password requirements policy

encryption.in_transit: TLS (version not specified; certificates from established third-party CA)
encryption.at_rest: Cloud provider key management service used (GCP KMS — CC6.1.5 tested, no exceptions)
encryption.key_management: GCP KMS; access to production environment access keys restricted to authorized individuals; encryption key access restricted to authorized personnel only (CC6.1.9 tested, no exceptions)

ci_cd_devops.source_control: Not named (source code management tool with MFA; source code changes logged, time-stamped, and attributed to author — CC8.1.3 / CC5.2.5 tested)
ci_cd_devops.branch_protection: Code repository branch rules configured to ensure every merge request to master branch requires additional approval. Developers cannot push to production without additional approval (CC8.1.2 / CC8.1.3 tested, no exceptions).
ci_cd_devops.iac: Baseline configurations retained within infrastructure as code; configuration management tool used for implementation and rollback capability (CC7.1.1 tested, no exceptions). Baseline configs reviewed annually.
ci_cd_devops.container_orchestration: Not disclosed (Cloud Functions serverless — no container orchestration mentioned)
ci_cd_devops.patch_management: Automatic patch management set up by CTO. Monthly patch management reviews via ITLC. Workstations configured for automatic updates and scanned daily for patch compliance (CC7.5.1 tested, no exceptions).

monitoring_logging.apm_tool: Not named (monitoring tool continuously monitors infrastructure performance for availability, efficiency, and reliability; generates alerts on predefined thresholds)
monitoring_logging.siem: GCP Security Command Center (explicitly named and visible in network diagram — receives output from Cloud Functions layer)
monitoring_logging.log_aggregation: Not named (logging enabled for administrative activities, logon attempts, data deletions, security configurations, permissions, roles at application and infrastructure level; CC7.2.1 tested, no exceptions)
monitoring_logging.alerting: Automated alerts configured for high and critical risk events; tracked in change-management/ticketing application

third_party_services.purpose: Data center / cloud infrastructure services (Firestore database, Cloud Functions compute, Firebase Authentication, Cloud Armor WAF, Cloud Load Balancing, Security Command Center, VPC, IDS/IPS, physical security, multi-AZ availability)

security_tools.antivirus: Anti-virus and Malware protection software installed on all production servers (continuous auto-scan) and Mac workstations/laptops. Anti-virus definitions updated weekly for servers (CC6.8.1 tested, no exceptions).
security_tools.vulnerability_scanning: Monthly automated vulnerability scans on infrastructure and applications. Self-assessment scan prior to every production release (unique practice not seen in other companies). High/critical findings remediated; follow incident response plan (CC7.1.2 / CC7.1.4 tested, no exceptions).
security_tools.penetration_testing: Annual external penetration testing by independent third party. High/critical findings remediated and tracked via incident management process (CC4.1.1 tested, no exceptions).

untestable: CC2.2.3 — No significant changes to people/roles during engagement period
CC3.2.1 — Cybersecurity insurance control could not be tested (no cyber incidents during engagement period)
CC6.5.2 — Customer data deletion could not be tested (no customer terminations during engagement period)
CC7.3.1 — Incident response effectiveness could not be tested (no security incidents during engagement period)
excluded_criteria: CC6.4 — Physical security excluded; GCP is responsible for data center physical security
A1.2 — Environmental protections excluded; Minutes AI has no office and all environmental controls managed by GCP
Processing Integrity — Management determined Processing Integrity trust services category is not relevant to the system
Privacy — Management determined Privacy trust services category is not relevant to the system
notable_controls: CC2.1.1 — IDS deployed and tested for unauthorized access detection
CC4.1.1 — Annual external penetration testing by independent third party confirmed tested
CC6.1.1 — MFA enforced for all administrative and remote access confirmed tested
CC6.2.2 — Quarterly user access reviews for all accounts including privileged users confirmed tested
CC7.4.2 — Daily backups with multi-availability zones and backup failure alerts confirmed tested
CC8.1.2 / CC8.1.3 — Branch protection (master branch) with required additional approval; source code tool requires MFA
CC3.1.1 — IT Leadership Committee (ITLC) meets weekly; RGEC meets quarterly for oversight
CC7.3.3 — Incident response plan tested periodically including root cause evaluation
CC7.1.1 — Infrastructure as code with configuration management tool for baseline/rollback confirmed tested
CC7.1.2 — Self-assessment vulnerability scan prior to every production release (unique — not present in other companies in this batch)

infrastructure.rationale: GCP directly named as subservice org (not via Supabase) with real named services: Cloud Functions, Firestore, Firebase Authentication, Cloud Armor, Cloud Load Balancing, Security Command Center. Multi-AZ with DR failover across all AZs. Real network diagram (not a placeholder). No named regions, no CDN, no secondary cloud. Serverless architecture (Cloud Functions) is a meaningful differentiator. Single cloud provider remains a concentration risk.

application_architecture.rationale: Serverless-heavy architecture visible (Cloud Functions in network diagram). Firebase Authentication is a named IdP. Firestore named as database. No languages or frameworks disclosed. Separate environments confirmed, branch protection enforced. The explicit serverless architecture is more architecturally defined than companies using generic Supabase/Kubernetes references.

data_layer.rationale: Firestore (GCP NoSQL) explicitly named in network diagram — specific and credible database disclosure. Daily backups, multi-AZ, data classification and retention policies, per-tenant segregation. No caching or data warehouse disclosed. No SQL/relational database named alongside Firestore — a single NoSQL store for a 600K+ user platform may indicate limitations.

security_posture.rationale: Cloud Armor (WAF) explicitly named and shown in network diagram — the only company in this batch with a named WAF. GCP Security Command Center named as SIEM-equivalent. IDS/IPS via GCP. Annual external pen testing. Monthly vulnerability scanning plus self-assessment scan before every production release (uniquely strong scanning cadence). Anti-malware on all systems. Cybersecurity insurance. Quarterly access reviews. Log protection. MFA enforced for all admin and remote access. The combination of named WAF + named SIEM-equivalent + pre-release scanning cadence is materially stronger than peers.

devops_maturity.rationale: Branch protection and code review enforced (master branch), separate environments, emergency change process, infrastructure-as-code with rollback, automatic patching with monthly ITLC review, self-assessment scan before each production release. CI/CD tool not named. Source control tool not named. Serverless architecture (Cloud Functions) suggests modern deployment patterns. Weekly ITLC meetings for operational oversight is a positive maturity indicator.

bcdr_readiness.rationale: BCDR policy documented, annually reviewed and tested across multiple loss scenarios, daily backups, multi-AZ failover by spinning up multiple servers across all GCP AZs. Incident response plan tested periodically (CC7.3.3 tested). No RTO/RPO stated. No multi-region failover. DR narrative confirmed in Section 5 (Other Information).

vendor_diversity.rationale: Only one subservice organization (GCP) named. All critical services — compute, database, authentication, WAF, security monitoring — are GCP-native services. No secondary cloud or CDN provider mentioned. Single-cloud concentration risk, though GCP's breadth of named services provides more internal resilience than Supabase-only companies.

overall.rationale: Minutes AI passes SOC 2 Type 2 with zero exceptions across Security, Availability, and Confidentiality trust criteria. The report is properly signed by Derrick Cai (Co-Founder & CEO). The network diagram is real and shows a specific GCP serverless architecture with Cloud Armor WAF, Firebase Authentication, Firestore, and Security Command Center — meaningfully more detailed than any other company in this batch. The pre-release vulnerability self-assessment is a uniquely strong DevOps security practice. The primary weaknesses are the 3-month audit window (minimum for Type 2), sole reliance on GCP, no disclosed RTO/RPO, and exclusion of Processing Integrity and Privacy criteria for an AI platform processing meeting audio/transcripts of potentially sensitive conversations.

red_flags: Complete dependency on a single subservice organization (GCP) with carve-out treatment — all physical security and data center controls rely on GCP

Processing Integrity and Privacy criteria explicitly excluded — notable for an AI platform processing meeting recordings and transcripts containing potentially sensitive conversations for 600,000+ users
yellow_flags: No CI/CD pipeline or source control tool named by product name
No CDN mentioned despite being a high-volume SaaS platform with 600K+ users on multiple platforms
Audit period is only 3 months (April 1 - July 1, 2025) — relatively short observation window for Type 2
No caching layer named despite 600K+ user scale (Firestore only as data store)
green_flags: Zero exceptions across all controls tested in the 3-month Type 2 period
Report properly signed by Derrick Cai (Co-Founder & CEO) — no placeholder text
Real network diagram showing specific GCP serverless architecture (Cloud Armor, Firebase Auth, Cloud Functions, Firestore, Security Command Center) — most detailed diagram in this batch
Cloud Armor (WAF) explicitly named — only company in this batch with a named WAF product
GCP Security Command Center named as security monitoring/SIEM-equivalent — only company in this batch with a named SIEM-class tool
Firebase Authentication as named IdP — explicit and credible identity provider disclosure
Firestore named as database — explicit database disclosure with diagram confirmation
Self-assessment vulnerability scan before every production release — uniquely strong scanning cadence not seen in other companies in this batch
Annual external penetration testing by independent third party confirmed tested
Monthly vulnerability scanning confirmed tested
MFA enforced for all administrative and remote access confirmed tested
Quarterly user access reviews including privileged accounts confirmed tested
Daily backups with multi-AZ configuration confirmed tested
Cybersecurity insurance maintained
BCDR plan documented, annually reviewed and tested across multiple loss scenarios
Branch protection and code review required prior to production deployment confirmed tested
Access revocation within one business day of termination confirmed tested
IT Leadership Committee (ITLC) meets weekly for operational oversight
Infrastructure-as-code with rollback capability confirmed tested
Incident response plan tested periodically (CC7.3.3)
key_observations: Minutes AI is the only company in this batch to name a WAF product (Cloud Armor) and a SIEM-equivalent (GCP Security Command Center) — both confirmed in the network diagram, making the security posture materially more verifiable than companies that mention these controls without naming the tool

The network diagram shows a serverless-first architecture (Cloud Functions as both compute and integration layer between Firestore and Security Command Center) — unusual and more modern than container-based or VM-based peers

Pre-release self-assessment vulnerability scanning before every production deployment is a unique control (CC7.1.2 mentions monthly scans 'with frequency adjusted as required'; system description explicitly mentions self-assessment scans prior to every production release) — this is the strongest vulnerability management cadence in the batch

The platform serves 600,000+ users with a 4.9 App Store rating across iOS, Android, macOS, Windows, and Web — this is a consumer-scale deployment, not just an enterprise B2B platform, which makes the exclusion of Privacy trust criteria more notable given the nature of meeting audio/transcript data

GCP is named directly as the subservice organization (not via Supabase as an intermediary) — this means GCP's own infrastructure controls are referenced without an additional layer of abstraction, providing slightly cleaner traceability

The RGEC (Risk and Governance Executive Committee) charter is reviewed and approved annually and the RGEC meets quarterly to evaluate key internal control measures — same governance structure as Tier One AI Labs (Company 2), consistent with Accorp Partners template

Processing Integrity and Privacy both excluded despite the platform processing meeting recordings, transcriptions, and AI-generated summaries — this scoping choice is more consequential for Minutes AI than for, say, an infrastructure tooling company
